1	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean
2	\chapter{Weil divisors on a smooth proper curve (RR.1)}
3	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}
4	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II \S6, pp.~130--137 +
5	% IV.1, pp.~294--296 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
6	% Stacks pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (divisors),
7	% 02ME (order at a point on a regular 1-dimensional scheme),
8	% 0BE0 (degree of a divisor on a curve), 0BE3 (principal divisors have
9	% degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve).
10	\section{Setup and motivation}
11	This chapter is the first of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
12	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
13	\[
14	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
15	  \,\colon\,
16	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
17	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
18	\]
19	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
20	divisor of a closed point on a curve (\texttt{RR.1}, this chapter); the
21	Riemann--Roch dimension formula \(\ell(D) = \deg D + 1\) for \(\deg D \geq 0\) on
22	a genus-\(0\) curve (\texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections argument identifying
23	\(\dim_k H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) via \(1\) and a non-constant function
24	(\texttt{RR.3}); the ``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)''
25	classification recovered as a degree-\(1\) morphism into \(\mathbb P^1\) via
26	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
27	Mathlib ships no \texttt{WeilDivisor} on a scheme. Adjacent pieces exist, but
28	none of them is the data structure we need: \texttt{MeromorphicOn.divisor}
29	(\texttt{Mathlib.Analysis.Meromorphic.Divisor}) is the order function of a
30	meromorphic function valued in a \emph{normed} field, not a formal sum of
31	codimension-\(1\) cycles on a scheme; \texttt{CommRing.Pic}
32	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) is the ring-level Picard group of
33	invertible modules under tensor product, with no scheme-level analogue;
34	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap}
35	(\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}) packages rational
36	maps but not divisors. Consequently this chapter, and the three siblings, are
37	\emph{project-bespoke}: the only Mathlib tools they rely on are
38	discrete-valuation rings, the function field, and the ambient scheme API.
39	The audit grounding this scope decision is in
40	\texttt{analogies/rrbridge-survey.md}.
41	This chapter establishes the formal-sum data type
42	\(\mathrm{Div}(X) = \bigoplus_{\substack{Z \subset X \\ \mathrm{codim} 1}} \mathbb Z\) on a
43	Noetherian integral scheme \(X\) satisfying Hartshorne's condition \((*)\), the
44	principal-divisor homomorphism \(\mathrm{div} \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \mathrm{Div}(X)\) on a
45	curve, the degree map \(\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \to \mathbb Z\) on a smooth proper
46	curve over an algebraically closed field, and the linear-equivalence
47	relation. The \emph{degree-zero of a principal divisor} statement
48	(\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}) is included for completeness; its proof
49	decomposes into a separate sub-build inside \texttt{RR.1} (it uses the
50	auxiliary lemma that any non-constant rational function on \(C\) defines a
51	finite morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\)). The line-bundle construction $\mathcal
52	O_C(D)\( and the associated cohomology computation \)H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$
53	live in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3}; the Riemann--Roch dimension
54	formula lives in \texttt{RR.2}; the genus-\(0\) classification proper lives in
55	\texttt{RR.4}.
56	Throughout this chapter, the standing convention is Hartshorne's condition
57	\((*)\):
58	% SOURCE QUOTE: "\((\ast)\) \(X\) is a noetherian integral separated scheme which
59	% is regular in codimension one."
60	\textit{Source: Hartshorne, II \S6, p.~130, the conditional \((*)\).}
61	\[
62	  (\ast)\qquad
63	  X \text{ is a noetherian integral separated scheme which is regular in
64	  codimension one.}
65	\]
66	For the degree map and for principal-divisor statements on a curve, we
67	additionally assume \(X\) is a complete (i.e.\ proper) nonsingular curve over
68	an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), where Hartshorne's condition \((*)\) is
69	automatic.
70	\paragraph{Standing hypothesis \((*)\) in the Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).}
71	Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) does not bundle Hartshorne's four-clause
72	\((\ast)\) as a single typeclass. The chapter pins \((*)\) to a \emph{layered}
73	typeclass set that the prover-agent threads through the Lean signatures in
74	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean}:
75	\begin{itemize}
76	  \item \textbf{Basic formal-sum layer} (carrier of \ref{def:prime_divisor} and
77	    \ref{def:codim1_cycles}, the additive group \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\), the degree
78	    \ref{def:divisor_degree}, the degree-homomorphism
79	    \ref{thm:divisor_degree_hom}, and \ref{def:divisor_closed_point}): the
80	    only Mathlib typeclass invoked is \texttt{[IsIntegral X]}
81	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme} via
82	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Properties}). Integrality of \(X\) already
83	    delivers \texttt{IrreducibleSpace X.carrier} (the carrier-level instance
84	    chain), so \(X\) has a unique generic point and the function field
85	    \texttt{X.functionField} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField})
86	    is a field; separatedness and noetherianness are \emph{not} required for
87	    the bare formal-sum data type.
88	  \item \textbf{Order/principal layer} (\ref{def:order_at_point},
89	    \ref{def:principal_divisor}, \ref{thm:principal_hom}): the Lean
90	    signature additionally threads
91	    \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X]}
92	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Noetherian}) and Hartshorne's
93	    regular-in-codimension-one clause. The latter has \emph{no} Mathlib
94	    typeclass shipping it directly; the chapter's pin is the project-side
95	    predicate ``for every \(x \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} = 1\) the
96	    stalk \(\mathcal O_{X,x}\) is a discrete valuation ring'', encodable as
97	    the conjunction
98	    $\forall x,\; \texttt{Order.coheight}\,x = 1 \Rightarrow
99	    \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing}\,(\mathcal O_{X,x})$. Iter-173+ may
100	    introduce a \texttt{Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne} predicate to
101	    abbreviate this; until then the order/principal lemmas thread the DVR
102	    hypothesis explicitly at each codim-1 point.
103	  \item \textbf{Curve layer} (\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}): the standing
104	    hypothesis tightens to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
105	    over \(\bar k\) with explicit integrality witness, namely
106	    \begin{quote}
107	      \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
108	       (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
109	       \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
110	       \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
111	    \end{quote}
112	    matching the standing typeclass set used by the rest of the project's
113	    curve API (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.Genus},
114	    \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity}). Hartshorne's \((*)\) is
115	    automatic for this layer (\texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian]} from finite type
116	    over a field; regular-in-codim-1 from smoothness; separatedness from
117	    properness; integrality threaded).
118	\end{itemize}
119	\noindent
120	The \texttt{[IsIntegral X]} hypothesis is the only one threaded through the
121	\emph{statement} of every Lean signature in
122	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean} that mentions a
123	function field, principal divisor, or order. The noetherian-and-DVR
124	content lives inside the proof bodies (sub-build), where the prover threads
125	the appropriate stronger hypothesis at the use site.
126	\section{Codim-\(1\) cycle group}
127	\begin{definition}
128	\leanok
129	[Prime divisor of a scheme]
130	  \label{def:prime_divisor}
131	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor}
132	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "prime divisor" under
133	  % condition \((*)\)) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
134	  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW (divisors).
135	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\). A \emph{prime
136	  % divisor} on \(X\) is a closed integral subscheme \(Y\) of codimension one."
137	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (definition of prime divisor).}
138	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). A \emph{prime divisor} on \(X\) is a closed integral
139	  subscheme \(Y \subset X\) of codimension one. Equivalently, by the standard
140	  generic-point correspondence (Hartshorne II \S6, p.~130, ``let $\eta \in
141	  Y\( be its generic point\ldots''), \)Y$ is determined by its generic point
142	  \(\eta \in X\): closed integral subschemes of codimension one of \(X\) are in
143	  natural bijection with points \(x \in X\) such that the closure
144	  \(\overline{\{x\}} \subset X\) has codimension one. The closure
145	  \(\overline{\{x\}}\), equipped with the \emph{reduced induced subscheme
146	  structure}, is automatically integral (irreducible, because the closure
147	  of a single point is irreducible, and reduced by construction); the only
148	  non-automatic condition is the codimension.
149	  \paragraph{Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).} The Lean structure
150	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor} bundles a point
151	  \texttt{point : X} together with a codimension-one witness, encoded via
152	  the Mathlib predicate
153	  \[
154	    \texttt{Order.coheight\ point\ =\ 1}
155	  \]
156	  on the specialisation preorder of the topological space underlying \(X\).
157	  Concretely, Mathlib's preorder on \texttt{X.carrier} is defined by
158	  ``\(x \leq y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x\)'' (i.e.\ \(x \in \overline{\{y\}}\);
159	  see \texttt{Mathlib.Topology.Specialization} and
160	  \texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension}). Under this convention the generic
161	  point of an integral scheme is the unique \emph{maximal} element of the
162	  preorder, so for the generic point \(\eta_Y\) of a prime divisor \(Y\) the
163	  only point strictly above \(\eta_Y\) is the generic point \(\eta_X\) of \(X\),
164	  and the chain \(\eta_Y < \eta_X\) has length one, giving
165	  \texttt{Order.coheight\ \(\eta_Y\)\ =\ 1}. The DVR/integrality content of
166	  ``the closure of \texttt{point} with the reduced subscheme structure is
167	  integral'' is automatic from \(\overline{\{\,\texttt{point}\,\}}\) being
168	  irreducible, so the Lean structure does not need a separate integrality
169	  field. The iter-172 placeholder \texttt{isCodim1AndIntegral : True} in
170	  \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean:84--90} is to be
171	  refined to \texttt{coheight : Order.coheight point = 1} (named field, type
172	  \texttt{Prop}) in the iter-173 prover step, retaining the same
173	  \texttt{point} field for downstream defeq.
174	  \paragraph{Reference predicates considered and rejected.}
175	  Two alternatives were considered and rejected at the iter-172
176	  lean-vs-blueprint-checker stage:
177	  \begin{itemize}
178	    \item \texttt{IsIntegral\ (X.closedSubschemeOfPoint\ x)}: rejected
179	      because Mathlib (\texttt{b80f227}) ships \emph{no}
180	      \texttt{closedSubschemeOfPoint} constructor. The closest available
181	      object is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData}
182	      (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf.Basic}), which packages
183	      a quasi-coherent ideal sheaf but does not produce a reduced induced
184	      subscheme on the closure of a point. Building that constructor is
185	      itself a Mathlib-upstream-scale project (Stacks tag 01J9 ``reduced
186	      induced scheme structure''), outside the scope of \texttt{RR.1}.
187	    \item A bundled \texttt{Scheme.IdealSheafData}-based prime-divisor
188	      carrier: rejected because the chapter only needs the generic-point
189	      witness for downstream order/principal-divisor consumption; carrying
190	      an \texttt{IdealSheafData} adds redundant data the chapter never
191	      uses.
192	  \end{itemize}
193	  The \texttt{Order.coheight}-based encoding is therefore the
194	  minimum-Mathlib-dependence pin compatible with the chapter's downstream
195	  needs.
196	\end{definition}
197	\begin{definition}
198	\leanok
199	[Codim-\(1\) cycle group / Weil divisor group of a scheme]
200	  \label{def:codim1_cycles}
201	  
202	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor}
203	  \uses{def:prime_divisor}
204	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "Weil divisor" under
205	  % condition \((*)\)) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
206	  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW.
207	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A \emph{Weil divisor} is an element of the free abelian
208	  % group \(\operatorname{Div} X\) generated by the prime divisors. We write a
209	  % divisor as \(D = \sum n_i Y_i\), where the \(Y_i\) are prime divisors, the
210	  % \(n_i\) are integers, and only finitely many \(n_i\) are different from
211	  % zero. If all the \(n_i \geq 0\), we say that \(D\) is \emph{effective}."
212	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (Weil divisor group).}
213	  Let \(X\) be a Noetherian integral separated scheme that is regular in
214	  codimension one (i.e.\ \(X\) satisfies \((*)\)). The \emph{Weil divisor group}
215	  \[
216	    \mathrm{Div}(X) \;=\; \bigoplus_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
217	    \mathbb Z \cdot Y
218	  \]
219	  is the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of \(X\)
220	  (\ref{def:codim1_cycles}); an element \(D = \sum n_i Y_i\) with finitely
221	  many nonzero coefficients \(n_i \in \mathbb Z\) is called a \emph{Weil
222	  divisor}, and is \emph{effective} when all \(n_i \geq 0\).
223	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
224	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor X} is given for an arbitrary
225	  scheme \texttt{X : Scheme} as the formal-sum data type
226	  \texttt{X.PrimeDivisor \(\to_0\) \(\mathbb Z\)}; integrality of \(X\) is
227	  \emph{not} required to form the additive group \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\) (it becomes
228	  necessary only at the order/principal-divisor layer where the function
229	  field appears). Hartshorne's \((*)\) is the standing convention in the
230	  prose but is not threaded into the typeclass arguments of this base
231	  declaration. See ``Standing hypothesis \((*)\) in the Lean encoding'' above
232	  for the per-layer typeclass discipline.
233	\end{definition}
234	\section{Order of a rational function at a prime divisor}
235	\begin{definition}
236	\leanok
237	[Order of a rational function at a prime divisor]
238	  \label{def:order_at_point}
239	  
240	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
241	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order}
242	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\) of a prime
243	  % divisor; "zero/pole of \(f\) along \(Y\)") (read from
244	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
245	  % Stacks pointer: tag 02ME.
246	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(Y\) is a prime divisor on \(X\), let \(\eta \in Y\) be its
247	  % generic point. Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{\eta, X}\) of \(X\) at
248	  % \(\eta\) is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field \(K\), the
249	  % function field of \(X\). We call the corresponding discrete valuation
250	  % \(v_Y\) the \emph{valuation of \(Y\)}."
251	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let \(f \in K^\ast\) be any nonzero rational function
252	  % on \(X\). Then \(v_Y(f)\) is an integer. If it is positive, we say \(f\) has
253	  % a \emph{zero} along \(Y\), of that order; if it is negative, we say \(f\)
254	  % has a \emph{pole} along \(Y\), of order \(-v_Y(f)\)."
255	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\)).}
256	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). For a prime divisor \(Y \subset X\) with generic
257	  point \(\eta \in Y\), the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a discrete
258	  valuation ring (by regularity in codimension one) with quotient field the
259	  function field \(K(X)\). For a nonzero rational function \(f \in K(X)^{\times}\)
260	  the \emph{order of \(f\) along \(Y\)} is the integer
261	  \[
262	    \ord_Y(f) \;:=\; v_Y(f) \;\in\; \mathbb Z,
263	  \]
264	  where \(v_Y\) is the normalised discrete valuation associated to
265	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\). If \(\ord_Y(f) > 0\), \(f\) has a \emph{zero of
266	  order \(\ord_Y(f)\) along \(Y\)}; if \(\ord_Y(f) < 0\), \(f\) has a \emph{pole
267	  of order \(-\ord_Y(f)\) along \(Y\)}.
268	  Specialisation to a curve. If \(C\) is a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)
269	  and \(P \in C\) is a closed point, then \(P\) is automatically a prime
270	  divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the \(1\)-dimensional
271	  scheme \(C\)); the local ring \(\mathcal O_{C, P}\) is a discrete valuation
272	  ring by smoothness, and \(\ord_P(f) = v_P(f)\) is the standard DVR
273	  valuation of \(f\) at \(P\).
274	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope (iter-175 pin, per analogist
275	  \texttt{dvr-rationalmap-order}).} The iter-175 Lean signature is
276	  \begin{quote}
277	    \texttt{noncomputable def order \{X : Scheme.\{u\}\} [IsIntegral X]\\
278	    \phantom{}\quad[IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)\\
279	    \phantom{}\quad[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]\\
280	    \phantom{}\quad(f : X.functionField) : \(\mathbb Z\)}
281	  \end{quote}
282	  with \emph{no} \(f \neq 0\) hypothesis (junk-on-\(f = 0\) convention,
283	  see below). The body is
284	  \[
285	    \texttt{order}\ Y\ f \;:=\;
286	      \texttt{WithZero.log}\ \bigl(
287	        \texttt{Ring.ordFrac}\ (X.\texttt{presheaf.stalk}\ Y.\texttt{point})\ f
288	      \bigr).
289	  \]
290	  \emph{Mathlib API path.}
291	  \begin{itemize}
292	    \item \texttt{Ring.ordFrac R : K \(\to\!\!*_0\) \(\mathbb Z^{m_0}\)}
293	      (with \(\mathbb Z^{m_0} = \texttt{WithZero (Multiplicative}\ \mathbb Z)\)),
294	      from \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic} (Stacks
295	      tag~\texttt{02MD}), is Mathlib's canonical monoid-with-zero hom
296	      extending the length-of-quotient valuation
297	      \texttt{Ring.ord} from a Noetherian Krull-dim-\(\leq 1\) ring to
298	      its fraction field via \texttt{Submonoid.LocalizationMap.lift\textsubscript{0}}.
299	      For a DVR-uniformised local ring its value on \(r \in R\) is the
300	      length of \(R/(r)\) (the DVR valuation); on \(a/b \in K\), it is
301	      \(\texttt{ord}(a) - \texttt{ord}(b)\). The hypotheses
302	      \texttt{[CommRing R] [Nontrivial R] [IsNoetherianRing R]\\
303	      [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 R]} are exactly the typeclasses the body
304	      synthesises from \([\texttt{IsIntegral}\ X]\) (delivers
305	      \texttt{IsDomain} hence \texttt{Nontrivial} on the stalk via
306	      \texttt{instIsDomainCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsIntegral},
307	      and \texttt{IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)
308	      X.functionField} via
309	      \texttt{instIsFractionRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafFunctionField}),
310	      from \([\texttt{IsLocallyNoetherian}\ X]\) (delivers
311	      \texttt{IsNoetherianRing} on the stalk via
312	      \texttt{instIsNoetherianRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsLocallyNoetherian}),
313	      and from the explicitly threaded
314	      \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]}.
315	    \item \texttt{WithZero.log : \(\mathbb Z^{m_0} \to \mathbb Z\)}
316	      from \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero} is Mathlib's
317	      canonical projection to the additive value group with
318	      junk-on-zero: \texttt{log\_zero}: \(\log 0 = 0\);
319	      \texttt{log\_mul}: \(\log(x \cdot y) = \log x + \log y\) for
320	      \(x, y \neq 0\). This makes the downstream additive identities
321	      \(v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)\), \(v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)\),
322	      \(v_Y(1) = 0\) available as named lemmas for
323	      \ref{def:order_at_point}.
324	  \end{itemize}
325	  \emph{Junk-on-\(f = 0\) convention.} Setting \(f = 0\) in
326	  \texttt{X.functionField} feeds \(0\) into
327	  \texttt{Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)} (zero of the
328	  monoid-with-zero hom; \texttt{Ring.ordFrac} maps \(0\) to
329	  \(0 \in \mathbb Z^{m_0}\)); then \texttt{WithZero.log 0 = 0} so
330	  \(\texttt{order}\ Y\ 0 = 0\). This is the canonical Mathlib junk
331	  choice; no separate \(f \neq 0\) hypothesis is threaded on the
332	  signature.
333	  \emph{In-tree gap-fill (project-side; Mathlib upstream opportunity).}
334	  Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) has no direct bridge from the
335	  topological condition \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\ Y.\texttt{point} = 1\)
336	  on \texttt{X.carrier} to the algebraic condition
337	  \(\texttt{Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)}\) on the
338	  stalk. The missing fact (Stacks tag~\texttt{02IZ} / \texttt{005X}) is
339	  the topological identity ``coheight of \(x\) in the scheme's
340	  specialisation preorder equals the height of the prime ideal
341	  corresponding to \(x\) in any affine chart''; the algebraic step
342	  ``ideal height \(=\) stalk Krull dim'' is in Mathlib as
343	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height}
344	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height}) and
345	  \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}
346	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}). The iter-175
347	  workaround is to thread the explicit instance
348	  \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]} on
349	  \texttt{order} (and downstream consumers); a future two-line
350	  Mathlib upstream PR pinning the coheight-height bridge would let
351	  the project drop the explicit argument in favour of synthesis from
352	  \texttt{coheight Y.point = 1} via the \texttt{PrimeDivisor} carrier.
353	\end{definition}
354	\section{Divisor of a closed point on a curve}
355	\begin{definition}
356	\leanok
357	[Divisor of a closed point on a curve]
358	  \label{def:divisor_closed_point}
359	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
360	  
361	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint}
362	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
363	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154):
364	  % "A prime divisor is just a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can
365	  % be written \(D = \sum n_i P_i\), where the \(P_i\) are closed points, and
366	  % \(n_i \in \mathbf Z\)."
367	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now we come to the study of divisors on curves. If \(X\)
368	  % is a nonsingular curve, then \(X\) satisfies the condition \((\ast)\) used
369	  % above, so we can talk about divisors on \(X\). A prime divisor is just
370	  % a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can be written $D = \sum n_i
371	  % P_i\(, where the \)P_i\( are closed points, and \)n_i \in \mathbf Z$."
372	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (``Divisors on Curves'').}
373	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over a field \(k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a
374	  closed point. The \emph{Weil divisor associated to \(P\)} is the element
375	  \[
376	    [P] \;:=\; 1 \cdot P \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(C)
377	  \]
378	  i.e.\ the prime divisor \(P\) with multiplicity one. The assignment is
379	  well-defined because the closed point \(P\) is automatically a codimension-\(1\)
380	  integral closed subscheme of the \(1\)-dimensional integral scheme \(C\).
381	  Conversely, every prime divisor on \(C\) is of this form, so an arbitrary
382	  divisor on \(C\) is a finite formal \(\mathbb Z\)-linear combination
383	  \(\sum n_i [P_i]\) of closed points.
384	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope (iter-174 pin).} The Lean signature
385	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint} accepts a
386	  scheme \texttt{C : Scheme.\{u\}}, a point \texttt{P : C}, and a
387	  hypothesis \texttt{(\_hP : IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C))}. It does
388	  \emph{not} typeclass-pin ``smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)'' at the
389	  carrier level, and it does \emph{not} thread the
390	  one-dimensional-integral-scheme content into the declaration head.
391	  Instead the iter-174 encoding is \emph{junk-defined} outside the
392	  codim-\(1\) regime:
393	  \[
394	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP\ :=\
395	      \begin{cases}
396	        \texttt{Finsupp.single}\ \langle P, h\rangle\ 1 &
397	          \text{if } h : \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1,\\
398	        0 & \text{otherwise.}
399	      \end{cases}
400	  \]
401	  On the off-branch --- where \texttt{P} is not a codimension-one point
402	  (e.g.\ \texttt{P} is the generic point of \texttt{C}, or a closed point
403	  of codimension \(\geq 2\) on a higher-dimensional scheme) ---
404	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} returns the zero divisor \(0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\). This
405	  convention lets the Lean signature avoid threading
406	  \texttt{[OneDimensional C]} / \texttt{[IsIntegral C]} typeclass payload
407	  at the declaration head; the well-definedness consumer must instead
408	  invoke the bridge equation lemmas
409	  \ref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_single} and \ref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
410	  to expose the two branches at the point of use.
411	  On the intended regime (one-dimensional integral scheme \(C\), \(P\) a
412	  closed point), the chapter's mathematical statement
413	  \([P] := 1 \cdot P \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) is recovered by combining
414	  \ref{def:divisor_closed_point} with the chain
415	  \(\texttt{IsClosed \{P\}} \Rightarrow \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1\)
416	  that the prover threads inside the consumer (using the
417	  one-dimensional-integrality content).
418	\end{definition}
419	\begin{lemma}
420	\leanok
421	[Bridge equation: \texttt{ofClosedPoint} in the codim-\(1\) regime]
422	  \label{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_single}
423	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint_eq_single}
424	  Let \(C : \texttt{Scheme.\{u\}}\) be a scheme and let \(P \in C\) be a
425	  point with \(\texttt{IsClosed}\,(\{P\} : \texttt{Set}\ C)\). If
426	  \(h : \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1\) (the codimension-one witness
427	  promoting \(P\) to a \texttt{PrimeDivisor} via \ref{def:divisor_closed_point}),
428	  then
429	  \[
430	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP \;=\;
431	      \texttt{Finsupp.single}\ \langle P, h\rangle\ 1
432	      \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(C).
433	  \]
434	\end{lemma}
435	\begin{proof}
436	  Unfolds the dependent-\texttt{if} in the definition of
437	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} (\ref{def:divisor_closed_point}) on the
438	  positive branch using the hypothesis \(h\).
439	\end{proof}
440	\begin{lemma}
441	\leanok
442	[Bridge equation: \texttt{ofClosedPoint} junk-branch]
443	  \label{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
444	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
445	  Let \(C : \texttt{Scheme.\{u\}}\) be a scheme and let \(P \in C\) be a
446	  point with \(\texttt{IsClosed}\,(\{P\} : \texttt{Set}\ C)\). If
447	  \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\ P \neq 1\) (the off-branch: \(P\) is not a
448	  codimension-one point of \(C\) --- e.g.\ \(P\) is the generic point of an
449	  integral scheme, or a higher-codim point of a higher-dimensional
450	  scheme), then
451	  \[
452	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP \;=\;
453	      0 \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(C).
454	  \]
455	\end{lemma}
456	\begin{proof}
457	  Unfolds the dependent-\texttt{if} in the definition of
458	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} (\ref{def:divisor_closed_point}) on the
459	  negative branch using the hypothesis
460	  \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\ P \neq 1\).
461	\end{proof}
462	\section{Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base}
463	\begin{definition}
464	\leanok
465	[Degree of a divisor on a curve over \(\bar k\)]
466	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
467	  \label{def:divisor_degree}
468	  
469	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree}
470	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
471	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154).
472	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE0.
473	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We define the \emph{degree} of \(D\) to be \(\sum n_i\)."
474	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (definition of degree on a
475	  nonsingular curve).}
476	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
477	  \(\bar k\). The \emph{degree} of a Weil divisor $D = \sum n_i [P_i] \in
478	  \mathrm{Div}(C)$ is the integer
479	  \[
480	    \deg(D) \;:=\; \sum_i n_i \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
481	  \]
482	  The definition uses that every closed point of \(C\) has residue field
483	  \(\bar k\) (so each prime divisor contributes degree one); over a general
484	  field \(k\) the same definition with the residue-field-degree weight
485	  \(\deg(D) := \sum_i n_i \, [\kappa(P_i) : k]\) recovers the
486	  \emph{geometric} degree, but the project's RR bridge needs only the
487	  \(\bar k\) specialisation.
488	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
489	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree} is given for an
490	  arbitrary scheme \texttt{X : Scheme.\{u\}} as the literal sum of
491	  coefficients via \texttt{Finsupp.sum D (fun \_ n => n)}; the typeclass
492	  set ``smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)'' is \emph{not} pinned on the
493	  base declaration. Mathematically this assigns to every Weil divisor
494	  \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\) on any scheme \(X\) the integer-valued sum of its
495	  multiplicities; for a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) this coincides
496	  with the curve-theoretic degree of the prose (every closed point has
497	  residue field \(\bar k\), so the residue-field-degree weight is identically
498	  one). The chapter's prose pins the curve hypothesis for clarity and to
499	  flag that, on a scheme with non-trivial residue-field extensions, the
500	  bare-sum signature does \emph{not} compute the geometric degree; the
501	  curve hypothesis is threaded at the call sites that consume
502	  \texttt{degree} for arithmetic statements
503	  (\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}, \texttt{RR.2}--\texttt{RR.4}).
504	\end{definition}
505	\begin{theorem}
506	\leanok
507	[The degree map is a group homomorphism]
508	  \label{thm:divisor_degree_hom}
509	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_hom}
510	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
511	  \(\bar k\). The degree map of \ref{def:divisor_degree} is a group
512	  homomorphism
513	  \[
514	    \deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \longrightarrow \mathbb Z,
515	    \qquad
516	    \deg(D_1 + D_2) \;=\; \deg(D_1) + \deg(D_2),
517	    \qquad
518	    \deg(-D) \;=\; -\deg(D).
519	  \]
520	\end{theorem}
521	\begin{proof}
522	  Immediate from the definition: \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) is the free abelian group on the
523	  set of closed points of \(C\), and \(\deg\) is the unique group homomorphism
524	  sending each generator \([P]\) to \(1 \in \mathbb Z\) (equivalently: the sum
525	  of the multiplicities). Both additivity and negation thus reduce to the
526	  corresponding identities for finite sums of integers.
527	\end{proof}
528	\section{Principal divisors}
529	\begin{lemma}
530	[Finite support of the order function — Hartshorne 6.1 / Stacks 02RV]
531	  \label{lem:rationalMap_order_finite_support}
532	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalMap_order_finite_support}
533	  % \lean{...} corrected iter-199 review: the iter-199 plan-phase
534	  % pin used the wrong namespace (".Scheme.") and the Lean theorem at
535	  % WeilDivisor.lean:357 sits in `namespace AlgebraicGeometry` only
536	  % (Scheme.RationalMap closed at L159). The theorem is also declared
537	  % `private` — sync_leanok may not resolve private decls cleanly. The
538	  % chapter pin is retained as documentation for the future-closed
539	  % version; the iter-199 plan agent's directive to consume this via
540	  % the `[IsNoetherian X]` strengthening remains the closure path.
541	  \uses{def:order_at_point, def:prime_divisor}
542	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Lemma~6.1, p.~131 (read from
543	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
544	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Lemma 6.1. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\), and let \(f \in K^\ast\)
545	  % be a nonzero function on \(X\). Then \(v_Y(f) = 0\) for all except finitely
546	  % many prime divisors \(Y\)."
547	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Lemma~6.1, p.~131 (a.k.a.\ Stacks 02RV).}
548	
549	  Let \(X\) be a Noetherian integral scheme that is regular in
550	  codimension one, and let \(f \in K(X)\) be a nonzero rational function.
551	  Then \(\{Y \in X^{(1)} \mid \ord_Y(f) \ne 0\}\) is finite.
552	\end{lemma}
553	
554	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. Let \(U = \operatorname{Spec} A\) be an open
555	% affine subset of \(X\) on which \(f\) is regular, \(f \in A\). Then for any
556	% prime divisor \(Y\) which meets \(U\), \(Y \cap U\) corresponds to a minimal
557	% prime ideal \(\mathfrak p\) of \(A\) containing \(f\). But these are finite
558	% in number (II, 3.10). On the other hand, \(X - U\) is a proper closed
559	% subset of \(X\), so it can contain only finitely many prime divisors of
560	% \(X\) [\dots]"
561	\begin{proof}
562	  \uses{def:order_at_point}
563	  Following Hartshorne II.6.1 / Stacks 02RV:
564	  \begin{enumerate}
565	  \item Pick a nonempty affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec} R \subset X\) on
566	    which \(f\) is regular. Then \(R\) is a Noetherian integral domain.
567	  \item For each prime divisor \(Y\) meeting \(U\), the corresponding
568	    prime \(\mathfrak p_Y\) is a height-1 prime of \(R\); the height-1 primes
569	    containing \(f \cdot 1\) are precisely the minimal primes of
570	    \((f)\), which by Krull's Hauptidealsatz form a finite set (Stacks tag
571	    00KZ / Atiyah--Macdonald 11.16). Of those, \(\ord_Y(f) \ne 0\) holds
572	    only at the finitely many minimal primes of \((f)\) over \(R\).
573	  \item The remaining prime divisors \(Y\) lie in the closed complement
574	    \(X \setminus U\), which is a proper closed subset of \(X\); the
575	    Noetherian topological structure of \(X\) (Stacks 04ZE) bounds the
576	    irreducible components of \(X \setminus U\) of codimension one in
577	    \(X\) by a finite number. For each such component, the stalk
578	    \(\mathcal O_{X, Y}\) is a DVR (regularity in codim 1) and \(f\) has
579	    finite valuation; together these contribute only finitely many
580	    nonzero values to the order function.
581	  \item Combining (2) and (3) gives the finiteness of the support.
582	  \end{enumerate}
583	  \emph{Hypothesis health.} The argument in (3) uses that \(X\) is
584	  Noetherian as a topological space (so \(X \setminus U\) has finitely
585	  many irreducible components). For \([X]\) only locally Noetherian +
586	  integral (without quasi-compactness), one can produce
587	  counter-examples with infinitely many disjoint codim-1 prime
588	  divisors outside \(U\). The Lean signature thus takes
589	  \texttt{[IsNoetherian X]} (or equivalently
590	  \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X] + [CompactSpace X]}). The hybrid
591	  WeilDivisor file pins the public-facing declaration under the
592	  weaker \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X]} hypothesis and exposes the
593	  \texttt{[CompactSpace X]} (or full Noetherian) strengthening
594	  as the substantive sub-claim that this lemma resolves.
595	\end{proof}
596	
597	\begin{definition}
598	\leanok
599	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:order_at_point, lem:rationalMap_order_finite_support}
600	[Principal divisor of a rational function]
601	  \label{def:principal_divisor}
602	  
603	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal}
604	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Lemma~6.1 + the definition of \(\operatorname{div}(f) = (f)\),
605	  % p.~131 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
606	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Lemma 6.1. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\), and let \(f \in K^\ast\)
607	  % be a nonzero function on \(X\). Then \(v_Y(f) = 0\) for all except finitely
608	  % many prime divisors \(Y\)."
609	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\) and let \(f \in K^\ast\).
610	  % We define the \emph{divisor of \(f\)}, denoted \((f)\), by
611	  % \((f) = \sum v_Y(f) \cdot Y\), where the sum is taken over all prime
612	  % divisors \(Y\) of \(X\). By the lemma, this is a finite sum, hence is a
613	  % divisor. Any divisor which is equal to the divisor of a function is
614	  % called a \emph{principal divisor}."
615	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Lemma~6.1 and the following definition,
616	  p.~131.}
617	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). For a nonzero rational function \(f \in K(X)^{\times}\),
618	  Hartshorne's Lemma~6.1 asserts that \(\ord_Y(f) = 0\) for all but finitely
619	  many prime divisors \(Y\) on \(X\); hence the formal sum
620	  \[
621	    \mathrm{div}(f) \;:=\; \sum_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
622	    \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(X)
623	  \]
624	  has finite support and is a well-defined Weil divisor, called the
625	  \emph{principal divisor of \(f\)}. On a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(\bar k\)
626	  the same definition specialises to
627	  \(\mathrm{div}(f) = \sum_{P \in C \text{ closed}} \ord_P(f) \cdot [P]\), the formal
628	  sum over closed points.
629	\end{definition}
630	\begin{theorem}
631	\leanok
632	[The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism]
633	  \label{thm:principal_hom}
634	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_hom}
635	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, the paragraph after the principal-divisor
636	  % definition, p.~131 (read from
637	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
638	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Note that if \(f, g \in K^\ast\), then \((f/g) = (f) - (g)\)
639	  % because of the properties of valuations. Therefore sending a function
640	  % \(f\) to its divisor \((f)\) gives a homomorphism of the multiplicative
641	  % group \(K^\ast\) to the additive group \(\operatorname{Div} X\), and the
642	  % image, which consists of the principal divisors, is a subgroup of
643	  % \(\operatorname{Div} X\)."
644	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (group-homomorphism remark).}
645	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). The principal-divisor map
646	  \(\mathrm{div} \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \mathrm{Div}(X)\) is a group homomorphism from the
647	  multiplicative group of nonzero rational functions to the additive group
648	  of Weil divisors. Concretely
649	  \[
650	    \mathrm{div}(fg) \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f) + \mathrm{div}(g),
651	    \qquad
652	    \mathrm{div}(f^{-1}) \;=\; -\mathrm{div}(f),
653	    \qquad
654	    \mathrm{div}(1) \;=\; 0.
655	  \]
656	\end{theorem}
657	\begin{proof}
658	  Each prime divisor \(Y\) on \(X\) contributes a discrete valuation \(v_Y\) on
659	  \(K(X)\) (\ref{def:principal_divisor}); by the standard DVR axioms,
660	  \(v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)\) and \(v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)\) for $f, g \in
661	  K(X)^{\times}\(, and \)v_Y(1) = 0$. Summing these identities over all prime
662	  divisors of \(X\) (a sum that has finite support on each factor by
663	  \ref{thm:principal_hom}, so the support of the sum is the union
664	  of the two finite supports and remains finite) gives the three displayed
665	  identities.
666	\end{proof}
667	\begin{theorem}
668	\leanok
669	[Principal divisors have degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve]
670	  \label{thm:principal_deg_zero}
671	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_degree_zero}
672	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Corollary~6.10, p.~138 (read from
673	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 155).
674	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE3.
675	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.10. \emph{A principal divisor on a complete
676	  % nonsingular curve \(X\) has degree zero. Consequently the degree function
677	  % induces a surjective homomorphism $\deg \colon \operatorname{Cl} X \to
678	  % \mathbf Z$.}"
679	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Corollary~6.10, p.~138.}
680	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
681	  \(\bar k\). For every nonzero rational function \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\) the
682	  associated principal divisor has degree zero:
683	  \[
684	    \deg(\mathrm{div}(f)) \;=\; 0 \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
685	  \]
686	\end{theorem}
687	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Let \(f \in K(X)^\ast\). If \(f \in k\), then
688	% \((f) = 0\), so there is nothing to prove. If \(f \notin k\), then the
689	% inclusion of fields \(k(f) \subset K(X)\) induces a finite morphism
690	% \(\varphi \colon X \to \mathbf P^1\). It is a morphism by (I, 6.12). We
691	% have \((f) = \varphi^\ast(\{0\} - \{\infty\})\). Since \(\{0\} - \{\infty\}\)
692	% is a divisor of degree 0 on \(\mathbf P^1\), we conclude that \((f)\) has
693	% degree 0 on \(X\)."
694	\begin{proof}
695	  Following Hartshorne's Corollary~6.10. If \(f \in \bar k \subset K(C)\) is
696	  a constant scalar, then \(f\) has no zeros or poles, so \(\mathrm{div}(f) = 0\) and
697	  \(\deg(\mathrm{div}(f)) = 0\). Otherwise the inclusion of fields
698	  \(\bar k(f) \subset K(C)\) exhibits \(K(C)\) as a finite extension of the
699	  rational function field \(\bar k(f) \cong \bar k(t)\), so the corresponding
700	  morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) is finite (by the
701	  smooth-proper-curve / function-field correspondence on which a curve is
702	  determined by its function field, classical I.6.12). The principal
703	  divisor \(\mathrm{div}(f)\) equals the pullback \(\varphi^{*}([0] - [\infty])\)
704	  along \(\varphi\) of the degree-\(0\) divisor \([0] - [\infty]\) on
705	  \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\); since pullback along a finite morphism
706	  multiplies degrees by \(\deg(\varphi)\), we conclude
707	  $\deg(\mathrm{div}(f)) = \deg(\varphi) \cdot \deg([0] - [\infty]) = \deg(\varphi)
708	  \cdot 0 = 0$.
709	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The two auxiliary statements ``the inclusion
710	  \(\bar k(f) \subset K(C)\) defines a finite morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\)''
711	  and ``pullback along a finite morphism of curves multiplies degrees by
712	  the degree of the morphism'' (Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.9, the
713	  multiplicativity of degree) are themselves separable sub-lemmas of
714	  \texttt{RR.1}; they are stated here for completeness and slotted to be
715	  closed in a follow-up iteration. The signature
716	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal\_degree\_zero}
717	  is recorded as the public face of this theorem and is depended on by
718	  the \texttt{RR.3}/\texttt{RR.4} chain.
719	\end{proof}

721	% NOTE (iter-190 plan-phase): added to host the Pin 2 corrective per
722	% `task_results/AlgebraicJacobian_RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso.lean.md`
723	% (iter-189). `Hom.poleDivisor` is being refactored to return the
724	% positive part of `principal (φ^* t_∞)` so that
725	% `lem:degree_via_pole_divisor` is mathematically correct (the principal
726	% divisor has degree 0 by `thm:principal_deg_zero`; only its
727	% positive part can have positive degree).
728	\begin{definition}
729	
730	
731	\leanok
732	[Positive part of a Weil divisor]
733	  \label{def:WeilDivisor_positivePart}
734	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart}
735	  \textit{Source: Project-bespoke (Hartshorne II.6.10 phrasing).}
736	  Let \(X\) be a scheme satisfying the codim-1 hypotheses of
737	  \ref{def:codim1_cycles}. For a Weil divisor
738	  \(D = \sum_Y n_Y \cdot Y \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\), the \emph{positive part}
739	  \((D)_0 \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\) is the Weil divisor
740	  \[
741	    (D)_0 \;:=\; \sum_{Y \,:\, n_Y > 0} n_Y \cdot Y.
742	  \]
743	  Concretely, \((D)_0\) is obtained from \(D\) by replacing each coefficient
744	  \(n_Y\) with \(\max(n_Y, 0)\). Equivalently, in the \(\Finsupp{\mathrm{PrimeDivisor}(X)}{\mathbb Z}\)
745	  presentation, the positive part is obtained either by the pointwise lattice
746	  join with the zero divisor — \(\,(D)_0 = D \sqcup 0\,\) — or, equivalently
747	  and more transparently for typeclass synthesis, by mapping the coefficients
748	  through the zero-preserving function \(n \mapsto n \sqcup 0\):
749	  \(\,(D)_0 = \mathtt{Finsupp.mapRange}\;(n \mapsto n \sqcup 0)\;D\). The two
750	  forms agree by the universal property of \(\mathtt{Finsupp.mapRange}\). The
751	  Lean implementation uses the \(\mathtt{mapRange}\) form to avoid the
752	  noncomputable \(\Finsupp.\mathtt{semilatticeSup}\) synthesis. The complementary \emph{negative part}
753	  \((D)_\infty\) is defined by \((D)_\infty := (-D)_0\), so that
754	  \(D = (D)_0 - (D)_\infty\) is the canonical decomposition into an
755	  effective (non-negative-coefficient) divisor minus an effective divisor.
756	  On a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(\bar k\), where the prime divisors
757	  are the closed points and the coefficients are integers, the positive part
758	  reduces to keeping only the closed points whose multiplicity is positive
759	  — i.e. precisely the zeros of any rational function whose principal
760	  divisor is \(D\), in the case \(D = \mathrm{div}(f)\).
761	\end{definition}
762	\begin{lemma}
763	
764	\leanok
765	[Degree of the positive part of \(\mathrm{div}(f)\) for a non-constant rational
766	function equals the function-field extension degree]
767	  \label{lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank}
768	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank}
769	  % NOTE (iter-194 refactor v2 `lane-i-localparameter-signature-v2`):
770	  % the iter-193 `hlp`-augmented signature was diagnosed STILL false by
771	  % the Lane I prover via counter-witness `K = K(C) = \bar k(u),
772	  % t = u(u-1)`. Iter-194 plan-phase refactor reshapes the Lean
773	  % signature per Option (b): drop the abstract `K` parameter and pin
774	  % `t \in K(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}) = (\text{ProjectiveLineBar
775	  % kbar}).left.functionField`; replace `hlp` with the stronger
776	  % uniformiser hypothesis
777	  %   `hLPUnif : \exists Y_0 \in \text{PrimeDivisor}(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}),
778	  %     order(Y_0, t) = 1 \land
779	  %     \forall Y, order(Y, t) > 0 \implies Y = Y_0`,
780	  % i.e. \(t\) has a unique simple zero on \(\mathbb P^1\). This rules
781	  % out the iter-193 counter-witness because (i) \(t = u(u-1)\) lives
782	  % in \(K(\mathbb P^1)\) but has TWO simple zeros (\(\{u = 0\}\),

784	  % function-field embedding `K = K(C)` is now disallowed at the type

786	  % `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank`

788	  % `localParameterAtInfty kbar = 1/u` (a Möbius transformation; simple
789	  % zero only at \(\infty\)) as a typed sorry owed iter-194+. Refactor
790	  % v2 net cost: \(+2\) typed-sorry typeclass instances on
791	  % `ProjectiveLineBar` (\verb|IsLocallyNoetherian|,
792	  % \verb|IsRegularInCodimensionOne|; both directly derivable from
793	  % `SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1` ⟹ regular ⟹ Noetherian + DVR-stalks).
794	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Proposition~6.9, p.~137 (multiplicativity
795	  % of degree under finite pullback) (read from
796	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154).
797	  % Stacks pointer: tag 02RW (pullback of divisors under finite
798	  % morphisms), tag 0BE6 (degree under finite morphism).
799	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.9. \emph{Let \(f \colon X \to Y\) be a finite
800	  % morphism of nonsingular curves. Then for any divisor \(D\) on \(Y\),
801	  % \(\deg(f^\ast D) = [K(X) : K(Y)] \cdot \deg D\).}"
802	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Proposition~6.9, p.~137.}
803	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over an
804	  algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), and let
805	  \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) be a non-constant morphism.
806	  Let \(t_\infty \in K(\mathbb P^1)\) be a local parameter at \(\infty \in \mathbb P^1\)
807	  (equivalently \(t_\infty = 1/u\) for the standard affine coordinate \(u\) on
808	  \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\)). Write \(f := \varphi^{\#} t_\infty
809	  \in K(C)\) for the pullback of \(t_\infty\) along \(\varphi\). Then \(f\) is
810	  nonzero (because \(\varphi\) is non-constant; equivalently \(\varphi^{-1}(\infty)\)
811	  is a proper closed subscheme of \(C\)) and the degree of the positive
812	  part of the principal divisor of \(f\) equals the function-field
813	  extension degree:
814	  \[
815	    \deg\bigl((\mathrm{div}(f))_0\bigr)
816	    \;=\; \bigl[\,K(C) \,:\, \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,\bigr]
817	    \;=\; \mathrm{Module.finrank}_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C).
818	  \]
819	  Equivalently, \((\mathrm{div}(f))_0 = \varphi^{*}[\infty]\) (the effective
820	  preimage divisor of \(\infty\)) and \(\deg(\varphi^{*}[\infty]) = \deg(\varphi)\).
821	\end{lemma}
822	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. The morphism \(f \colon X \to Y\) is finite,
823	% so locally on \(Y\) corresponds to a finite extension of Dedekind domains
824	% \(A \subset B\). For each closed point \(P \in Y\) the ramification--inertia
825	% formula gives \(\sum_{Q | P} e_Q f_Q = [K(X) : K(Y)]\), where the sum runs
826	% over closed points \(Q \in X\) above \(P\). Summing this identity over
827	% \(P \in Y\) using the affine cover and noting that \(D = \sum_P n_P [P]\)
828	% gives \(\deg(f^\ast D) = \sum_{P} n_P \sum_{Q | P} e_Q f_Q
829	% = \sum_P n_P [K(X) : K(Y)] = [K(X) : K(Y)] \deg(D)\)."
830	\begin{proof}
831	  Following Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.9 specialised to the divisor
832	  \(D = [\infty]\) on \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\). A non-constant morphism
833	  between smooth proper curves over \(\bar k\) is automatically finite (the
834	  image is a closed irreducible subset that is not a point and not empty,
835	  hence all of \(\mathbb P^1\); a surjective morphism between smooth proper
836	  irreducible curves of equal dimension is proper + quasi-finite, hence
837	  finite; cf.\ Hartshorne II.6.8). Hence the function-field extension
838	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\) is finite of degree
839	  \(d := [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)] = \dim_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C)\).
840	  Pick an affine open \(\mathrm{Spec}(A) \subset \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) containing
841	  \(\infty\); explicitly take \(A = \bar k[t_\infty]\) the standard chart at
842	  \(\infty\), with \(\infty\) corresponding to the maximal ideal
843	  \(\mathfrak m_\infty = (t_\infty) \subset A\). The preimage
844	  \(\mathrm{Spec}(B) := \varphi^{-1}(\mathrm{Spec}(A))\) is an affine open of \(C\)
845	  with \(B\) a finite (and integrally closed, by \(C\) smooth) extension of
846	  \(A\); both are Dedekind domains. Apply
847	  \texttt{Ideal.sum\_ramification\_inertia} (Mathlib,
848	  \texttt{Mathlib.NumberTheory.RamificationInertia.Basic}) to the
849	  ring-theoretic extension \(A \to B\) at the maximal ideal
850	  \(\mathfrak m_\infty\):
851	  \[
852	    \sum_{Q \,\colon\, \mathfrak m_\infty \subset Q} e_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty} \cdot f_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty}
853	    \;=\; \dim_{A / \mathfrak m_\infty}\!\bigl(B / \mathfrak m_\infty B\bigr)
854	    \;=\; \dim_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C) \;=\; d,
855	  \]
856	  where the second equality uses
857	  \texttt{Ideal.finrank\_quotient\_map} (which identifies the residue-field
858	  quotient with the function-field extension degree for a Dedekind extension
859	  at a maximal ideal). The closed points \(Q \subset \mathrm{Spec}(B)\) above
860	  \(\mathfrak m_\infty\) are precisely the closed points
861	  \(P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty) \subset C\); the ramification index
862	  \(e_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty}\) is the order \(\ord_P(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty)\)
863	  of the pulled-back local parameter at \(P\); the residue degree
864	  \(f_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty}\) is \([\kappa(P) : \bar k] = 1\) (every closed
865	  point of \(C\) over \(\bar k\) has residue field \(\bar k\)). Therefore
866	  \[
867	    \sum_{P \,\in\, \varphi^{-1}(\infty)} \ord_P(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty) \;=\; d
868	    \;=\; \mathrm{Module.finrank}_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C).
869	  \]
870	  The left-hand side equals the degree of the positive part of
871	  \(\mathrm{div}(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty)\), because: at every closed point
872	  \(P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)\) the order is \(\geq 1\) (pullback of a local
873	  parameter under a finite morphism is itself a uniformiser-power, hence
874	  has positive order at every preimage); at every closed point
875	  \(P \in C \setminus \varphi^{-1}(\infty)\) the rational function
876	  \(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty\) is regular and nonzero (because \(t_\infty\) is
877	  regular and nonzero on \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\)), hence the
878	  order at such a \(P\) is \(\geq 0\); the points where the order is
879	  \emph{strictly} positive are exactly the points of \(\varphi^{-1}(\infty)\).
880	  Therefore
881	  \(\,(\mathrm{div}(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty))_0 = \sum_{P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
882	  \ord_P(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty) \cdot [P] = \varphi^{*}[\infty]\,\), and
883	  taking degree completes the proof.
884	\end{proof}
885	\section{Divisor class group}
886	We follow Hartshorne II.6 (pp.~131--132) and define the divisor class group
887	of a curve as the cokernel of the principal-divisor homomorphism. This is
888	the \emph{Picard group} of \(C\) in the curve-specific sense; the line-bundle
889	interpretation \(\Cl(C) \cong \Pic(C)\) requires the construction of
890	\(\mathcal O_C(D)\), which lives in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3} and is
891	out of scope here.
892	\begin{definition}
893	\leanok
894	[Linear equivalence of divisors]
895	  \label{def:linear_equivalence}
896	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.LinearEquivalence}
897	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~131 (definition of linear equivalence
898	  % and of \(\operatorname{Cl} X\)) (read from
899	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
900	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\). Two divisors \(D\)
901	  % and \(D'\) are said to be \emph{linearly equivalent}, written \(D \sim D'\),
902	  % if \(D - D'\) is a principal divisor. The group \(\operatorname{Div} X\) of
903	  % all divisors divided by the subgroup of principal divisors is called
904	  % the \emph{divisor class group} of \(X\), and is denoted by
905	  % \(\operatorname{Cl} X\)."
906	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (linear equivalence and
907	  \(\operatorname{Cl} X\)).}
908	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). Two Weil divisors \(D, D' \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\) are
909	  \emph{linearly equivalent}, written \(D \sim D'\), if and only if there
910	  exists \(f \in K(X)^{\times}\) with
911	  \[
912	    D - D' \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f) \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(X).
913	  \]
914	  The relation \(\sim\) is an equivalence relation on \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\) (the
915	  reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity all transport from the analogous
916	  identities \(\mathrm{div}(1) = 0\), \(\mathrm{div}(f^{-1}) = -\mathrm{div}(f)\), $\mathrm{div}(fg) =
917	  \mathrm{div}(f) + \mathrm{div}(g)$ via \ref{thm:principal_hom}); the corresponding
918	  quotient group \(\Cl(X) := \mathrm{Div}(X) / \mathrm{im}(\div)\) is the
919	  \emph{divisor class group} of \(X\). On a smooth proper curve \(C\) over
920	  \(\bar k\), \ref{thm:principal_deg_zero} implies that the degree map
921	  \(\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \to \mathbb Z\) descends to a homomorphism
922	  \(\deg \colon \Cl(C) \to \mathbb Z\).
923	\end{definition}
924	\section{Out of scope}

926	are sequenced into the sibling sub-build chapters:

928	  \item The line-bundle construction \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) associated to a Weil
929	    divisor \(D\), the calculation of its global sections, and the
930	    identification \(\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')\) when
931	    \(D \sim D'\), lives in \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
932	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added).
933	  \item The Riemann--Roch dimension formula
934	    \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) and its genus-\(0\) specialisation
935	    \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\) live in \texttt{RR.2}
936	    (sibling chapter \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RRFormula.tex}, to be added).
937	  \item The classification ``a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
938	    curve over \(\bar k\) with \(g = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)''
939	    (Hartshorne Example~IV.1.3.5) is the headline of \texttt{RR.4}
940	    (sibling chapter \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalIsoP1.tex}, to be added)
941	    and assembles \texttt{RR.1}/\texttt{RR.2}/\texttt{RR.3} into the
942	    \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} morphism into \(\mathbb P^1\) together
943	    with its degree-\(1\) identification.
944	  \item \emph{Cartier divisors} (locally principal divisors on a possibly
945	    singular scheme) are out of scope: the project's curves are smooth, so
946	    Weil and Cartier divisors coincide, and the simpler Weil form suffices.
947	    A future Mathlib upstream PR could introduce the Cartier formalism.
948	  \item \emph{Higher-codimension cycles} (the Chow group
949	    \(\bigoplus_i \mathrm{CH}^i(X)\)) are out of scope: this chapter is
950	    strictly curve-focused.
951	  \item The proof of \ref{thm:principal_deg_zero} factors through two
952	    auxiliary statements (``finite morphism induced by a non-constant

954	    pullback'', Hartshorne II.6.9) which are themselves separable
955	    sub-lemmas of \texttt{RR.1}; they are flagged in the proof body but
956	    deliberately not unfolded as standalone declarations here.
957	\end{itemize}

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface \(S\) over a field --- such as \(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1\)
28	or the product \(C \times C\) of a smooth projective curve \(C\) with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension \(2\). To extend a rational map
30	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from \(S\) to an abelian variety across a codimension-\(2\) closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} \(2\), hence a rational function with no codimension-\(1\) poles on a neighbourhood of \(x\)
33	extends across \(x\). The depth \(\geq 2\) inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring \(R\) one has \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\),
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension \(d\) admits an \(R\)-regular sequence of length \(d\) generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\), i.e.\ \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(Corollary~REF below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its \(\text{Ext}\) reformulation
47	    (Definition~REF and Lemma~REF),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (Definition~REF),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma
51	    (Lemma~REF),
52	  \item the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula proper
53	    (Theorem~REF), and
54	  \item the corollary regular \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay
55	    (Corollary~REF).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	A short closing paragraph (Section~REF) explains how A.4.a
58	consumes Corollary~REF to push a codim-1 extension across a
59	codim-2 point.
60	
61	\section{Depth of a module over a local ring}
62	\label{sec:ab_depth}
63	
64	\begin{definition}
65	
66	\leanok
67	  [Depth of a module]
68	  \label{def:depth}
69	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth}
70	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth)
71	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17748-L17759).
72	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
73	  % "Let \(R\) be a ring, and \(I \subset R\) an ideal. Let \(M\) be a finite \(R\)-module.
74	  %  The {\it \(I\)-depth} of \(M\), denoted \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is defined as follows:
75	  %  \begin{enumerate}
76	  %  \item if \(IM \not = M\), then \(\text{depth}_I(M)\) is the supremum in
77	  %  \(\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences in \(I\),
78	  %  \item if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\).
79	  %  \end{enumerate}
80	  %  If \((R, \mathfrak m)\) is local we call \(\text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M)\) simply
81	  %  the {\it depth} of \(M\)."
82	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth).}
83	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(I \subseteq R\) be an ideal, and let \(M\) be a finitely
84	  generated \(R\)-module. The \emph{\(I\)-depth} of \(M\), written \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is the
85	  supremum in \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences contained
86	  in \(I\), provided \(IM \neq M\); if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\). When
87	  \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is a Noetherian local ring we abbreviate
88	  \(\text{depth}(M) := \text{depth}_{\mathfrak{m}}(M)\) and call this simply the
89	  \emph{depth} of \(M\). The depth of the ring \(R\) itself is \(\text{depth}(R)\), the depth
90	  of \(R\) as a module over itself.
91	\end{definition}
92	
93	Recall that a sequence \(f_1, \dots, f_r \in R\) is called \emph{\(M\)-regular} if
94	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_r)M \neq 0\) and for each \(i\), the element \(f_i\) is a non-zero-divisor on
95	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_{i-1})M\). The empty sequence is regular on \(M\) iff \(M \neq 0\) (it is
96	\emph{not} regular on the zero module, but the convention \(\text{depth}(0) = \infty\)
97	above is the usual one).
98	
99	The two practical computations of depth are the regular-sequence definition (used to
100	establish a \emph{lower} bound) and the \(\text{Ext}\) characterisation (used to establish an
101	\emph{upper} bound and to prove the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequality).
102	
103	\begin{lemma}
104	
105	\leanok
106	  [Depth via \(\text{Ext}\)]
107	  \label{lem:depth_via_ext}
108	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index}
109	  
110	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext)
111	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17845-L17885).
112	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
113	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\).
114	  %  Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module. Then \(\text{depth}(M)\)
115	  %  is equal to the smallest integer \(i\) such that
116	  %  \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\) is nonzero."
117	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext).}
118	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring with residue field
119	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak{m}\) and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely generated \(R\)-module. Then
120	  \[
121	    \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \inf\bigl\{\, i \in \mathbb{N} \;\bigm|\; \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \,\bigr\}.
122	  \]
123	\end{lemma}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
126	% "Let \(\delta(M)\) denote the depth of \(M\) and let \(i(M)\) denote
127	%  the smallest integer \(i\) such that \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\)
128	%  is nonzero. We will see in a moment that \(i(M) < \infty\).
129	%  By Lemma REF we have
130	%  \(\delta(M) = 0\) if and only if \(i(M) = 0\), because
131	%  \(\mathfrak m \in \text{Ass}(M)\) exactly means
132	%  that \(i(M) = 0\). Hence if \(\delta(M)\) or \(i(M)\) is \(> 0\), then we may
133	%  choose \(x \in \mathfrak m\) such that (a) \(x\) is a nonzerodivisor
134	%  on \(M\), and (b) \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta(M) - 1\).
135	%  Consider the long exact sequence
136	%  of Ext-groups associated to the short exact sequence
137	%  \(0 \to M \to M \to M/xM \to 0\) by Lemma REF:
138	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
139	%  Since \(x \in \mathfrak m\) all the maps $\Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)
140	%  \to \Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)$ are zero, see
141	%  Lemma REF.
142	%  Thus it is clear that \(i(M/xM) = i(M) - 1\). Induction on
143	%  \(\delta(M)\) finishes the proof."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	  
148	  
149	\leanok
150	  Write \(\delta := \text{depth}(M)\) and \(i := \inf\{ j : \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \}\).
151	  The base case \(\delta = 0\) is exactly \(\text{Hom}_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0\) (some element of
152	  \(M\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\), i.e.\ \(\mathfrak{m} \in \text{Ass}(M)\), i.e.\
153	  \(\mathfrak{m}\) contains a zero-divisor on \(M\)), which is \(i = 0\). For the inductive step
154	  pick \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) a non-zero-divisor on \(M\) with
155	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta - 1\). The short exact sequence
156	  \(0 \to M \xrightarrow{x} M \to M/xM \to 0\) induces a long exact sequence of $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa,
157	  -)\(. Multiplication by \)x\( annihilates each \)\text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)$ (because
158	  \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) and \(\kappa\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\)), so the long exact
159	  sequence breaks into short pieces giving
160	  \(\text{Ext}^{j-1}_R(\kappa, M/xM) \cong \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)\) at the relevant
161	  indices, and the smallest non-vanishing index drops by \(1\). By induction
162	  \(i(M/xM) = i - 1 = \delta - 1\), so \(i = \delta\).
163	\end{proof}

165	\section{Projective dimension}
166	\label{sec:ab_pd}
167	
168	\begin{definition}
169	
170	\leanok
171	  [Projective dimension]
172	  \label{def:projective_dimension}
173	  \lean{Module.projectiveDimension}
174	  % NOTE: Mathlib pinned at b80f227 may already expose this as
175	  % `Module.projectiveDimension` via
176	  % `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.ProjectiveDimension` /
177	  % `Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.ProjectiveResolution`; the prover should re-export
178	  % rather than re-define if the API is present. This block fixes the
179	  % mathematical content the prover must match.
180	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(M\) be an \(R\)-module. The \emph{projective
181	  dimension} of \(M\), written \(\text{pd}_R(M)\), is the infimum in
182	  \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths \(n\) of projective resolutions
183	  \[
184	    0 \longrightarrow P_n \longrightarrow P_{n-1} \longrightarrow \cdots
185	    \longrightarrow P_1 \longrightarrow P_0 \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
186	  \]
187	  with each \(P_i\) a projective \(R\)-module. We say \(M\) has \emph{finite projective dimension}
188	  if \(\text{pd}_R(M) < \infty\), and \emph{infinite projective dimension} otherwise. By
189	  convention \(\text{pd}_R(0) = -\infty\) (some references); we use \(\text{pd}_R(0) = 0\) here
190	  (the empty resolution \(0 \to 0\) of length \(0\)), but the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula in
191	  Theorem~REF concerns only nonzero \(M\).
192	  In the local Noetherian setting a finitely generated \(R\)-module of finite projective
193	  dimension admits a \emph{minimal finite free resolution}: a resolution with each \(P_i\)
194	  free of finite rank and each transition map \(P_{i+1} \to P_i\) landing in \(\mathfrak{m} P_i\).
195	\end{definition}
196	
197	The two basic facts about \(\text{pd}_R(M)\) needed downstream are:
198	\begin{itemize}
199	  \item \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) iff \(M\) is projective (in the local Noetherian setting,
200	    iff \(M\) is free);
201	  \item if \(0 \to K \to P \to M \to 0\) is a short exact sequence with \(P\) projective, then
202	    \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_R(K) + 1\) provided \(K \neq 0\).
203	\end{itemize}
204	Both are standard homological algebra and are recorded under
205	\texttt{Module.projectiveDimension\_succ\_iff} or similar in Mathlib.
206	
207	\section{Depth on a short exact sequence}
208	\label{sec:ab_depth_ses}
209	
210	\begin{lemma}
211	
212	\leanok
213	  [Depth on a short exact sequence]
214	  \label{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}
215	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_of_short_exact}
216	  
217	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses)
218	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17887-L17920).
219	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
220	  % "Let \(R\) be a local Noetherian ring. Let \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\)
221	  %  be a short exact sequence of nonzero finite \(R\)-modules.
222	  %  \begin{enumerate}
223	  %  \item
224	  %  \(\text{depth}(N) \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N'), \text{depth}(N'')\}\)
225	  %  \item
226	  %  \(\text{depth}(N'') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N') - 1\}\)
227	  %  \item
228	  %  \(\text{depth}(N') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N'') + 1\}\)
229	  %  \end{enumerate}"
230	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses).}
231	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let
232	  \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of nonzero finitely generated
233	  \(R\)-modules. Then
234	  \begin{enumerate}
235	    \item \(\text{depth}(N) \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N'),\; \text{depth}(N'')\bigr)\);
236	    \item \(\text{depth}(N'') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N') - 1\bigr)\);
237	    \item \(\text{depth}(N') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N'') + 1\bigr)\).
238	  \end{enumerate}
239	\end{lemma}
240	
241	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
242	% "Use the characterization of depth using the Ext groups
243	%  \(\Ext^i(\kappa, N)\), see Lemma REF,
244	%  and use the long exact cohomology sequence
245	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
246	%  from Lemma REF."
247	\begin{proof}
248	  
249	  
250	  
251	  
252	\leanok
253	  By Lemma~REF, \(\text{depth}(N')\) (resp.\ \(\text{depth}(N)\),
254	  \(\text{depth}(N'')\)) is the smallest index at which \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\) is
255	  nonzero on \(N'\) (resp.\ \(N\), \(N''\)). Apply the long exact \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\)
256	  sequence to \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\):
257	  \[
258	    \cdots \to \text{Ext}^{i-1}_R(\kappa, N'')
259	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N')
260	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N)
261	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N'')
262	    \to \text{Ext}^{i+1}_R(\kappa, N') \to \cdots
263	  \]
264	  Each of the three inequalities is then a direct read-off of the fact that if two
265	  of the three modules in a length-3 chunk vanish then the middle vanishes (for (1)) /
266	  the boundary terms vanish (for (2),(3)). Compare with the diagonal vanishing argument in
267	  the proof of Lemma~REF.
268	\end{proof}

271	\label{sec:ab_main}

348	%  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\).
349	%  Till now depths have all been depths as \(R\) modules, but we observe that
350	%  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) and similarly for \(R/xR\).
351	%  By induction hypothesis we see that the
352	%  Auslander-Buchsbaum formula holds for \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\). Since the
353	%  depths of both \(R/xR\) and \(M/xM\) have decreased by one and the projective
354	%  dimension has not changed we conclude."
355	\begin{proof}
356	  
357	  
358	\leanok
359	  We induct on \(\text{depth}(M)\).
360	
361	  \emph{Base case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).} Let \(e := \text{pd}_R(M)\) and pick a minimal
362	  finite free resolution
363	  \[
364	    0 \longrightarrow R^{n_e} \longrightarrow R^{n_{e-1}} \longrightarrow
365	    \cdots \longrightarrow R^{n_0} \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
366	  \]
367	  with all transition maps having matrix coefficients in \(\mathfrak{m}\) (this is the
368	  ``minimal-resolution-after-trimming-trivial-summands'' normalisation, cf.\ Stacks
369	  \texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}). On the one hand, the ``what is exact'' criterion
370	  (Stacks tag 00MF, \texttt{proposition-what-exact}, expressing exactness of a sequence of
371	  finite free modules in terms of depths of ideals of \(r\)-minors) gives
372	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, splitting the resolution into short exact
373	  sequences
374	  \[
375	    0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to K_{e-2} \to 0, \quad
376	    0 \to K_{e-2} \to R^{n_{e-2}} \to K_{e-3} \to 0, \quad \dots, \quad
377	    0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0,
378	  \]
379	  and applying Lemma~REF iteratively,
380	  yields the chain of inequalities
381	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1\),
382	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2\), \dots,
383	  \(\text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e-1)\), and finally
384	  \(\text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e\). Since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we deduce
385	  \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\). Combined with the previous inequality, $\text{depth}(R) = e =
386	  \text{pd}_R(M) + 0 = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)$.
387	
388	  \emph{Inductive step \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\).} We claim there exists \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\)
389	  which is a non-zero-divisor on both \(R\) and \(M\). Indeed: either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\), in
390	  which case \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the ``what is exact'' criterion applied to the start
391	  of a minimal resolution, or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which case \(M\) is finite free and
392	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\). Either way both \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) and
393	  \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), hence \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by
394	  Lemma~REF, so a common non-zero-divisor $x \in
395	  \mathfrak{m}\( exists. Take a minimal finite free resolution of \)M$ as above; the snake
396	  lemma applied to multiplication by \(x\) gives a minimal finite free resolution
397	  \[
398	    0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \cdots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to M/xM \to 0
399	  \]
400	  of \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\), of the same length \(e\). So
401	  \(\text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) = \text{pd}_R(M)\). By Stacks
402	  \texttt{lemma-depth-drops-by-one}, both \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
403	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\), and one verifies
404	  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) (regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}\)
405	  correspond to regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}/(x)\)). Apply the inductive hypothesis to
406	  \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\):
407	  \[
408	    \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) + \text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(R/xR).
409	  \]
410	  Substituting yields
411	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + (\text{depth}(M) - 1) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\), i.e.\
412	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M) = \text{depth}(R)\), as required.
413	\end{proof}
414	
415	\begin{lemma}
416	
417	\leanok
418	  [Auslander--Buchsbaum: inductive step \(\text{pd} = k+1\)]
419	  \label{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}
420	  \lean{RingTheory.auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}
421	  % NOTE iter-199 review (per lean-vs-blueprint-checker ab-iter199 F-3):
422	  % the pinned declaration at AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean:1398 is `private`.

431	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum),
432	  induction-on-\(\text{pd}\) re-organisation; cf.\ Matsumura, Theorem 19.1.}
433	
434	  Inductive-step variant pinning the project-internal helper used by
435	  \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}: for a Noetherian local ring
436	  \((R, \mathfrak m)\), a nonzero finite \(R\)-module \(M\) of projective
437	  dimension \(k+1\), and the AB formula known for projective dimension
438	  \(\leq k\), one has
439	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M) = \text{depth}(R)\).
440	\end{lemma}
441	\begin{proof}
442	  \uses{lem:depth_drops_by_one}
443	  Replay of the induction step in the main proof's
444	  \emph{inductive step} paragraph (\cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum},
445	  proof block), using \cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one} for the
446	  depth-drop on both \(R\) and \(M\) after quotienting by an
447	  \(M\)-regular \(x \in \mathfrak m\), plus a snake-lemma carving of
448	  the minimal resolution to extract the \(R/(x)\)-minimal resolution
449	  of \(M/xM\) of the same length. The four ingredients are:
450	  \begin{enumerate}
451	    \item Minimal-resolution carving (Stacks
452	      \texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}): every finite free
453	      resolution of a finite module over a Noetherian local ring can
454	      be trimmed to a minimal one of the same projective dimension.
455	    \item ``What is exact'' criterion (Stacks 00MF /
456	      \texttt{proposition-what-exact}): characterises exactness in
457	      terms of depths of \(r\)-minors. Used to extract a common
458	      non-zero-divisor \(x \in \mathfrak m\) for both \(R\) and \(M\).
459	    \item Snake lemma applied to multiplication-by-\(x\) on the
460	      minimal resolution: produces a minimal resolution of \(M/xM\)
461	      over \(R/(x)\) of the same length, so
462	      \(\text{pd}_{R/(x)}(M/xM) = \text{pd}_R(M)\).
463	    \item Depth drops by one
464	      (\cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one}): once an \(M\)-regular element
465	      \(x \in \mathfrak m\) is fixed,
466	      \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\) and likewise on \(R\).
467	  \end{enumerate}
468	  Combining (1)-(4) with the inductive hypothesis on \(M/xM\) over
469	  \(R/(x)\) closes the inductive step. Pieces (1)-(3) remain Mathlib
470	  gaps as of \texttt{b80f227}; piece (4) is now formalised
471	  axiom-clean (\cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one}).
472	\end{proof}

474	\begin{lemma}
475	
476	\leanok
477	  [Depth drops by one under a regular element]
478	  \label{lem:depth_drops_by_one}
479	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_quotSMulTop_succ_eq_depth_of_isSMulRegular}
480	  \uses{def:depth, lem:depth_via_ext}
481	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LL (\texttt{lemma-depth-drops-by-one}).}
482	
483	  For a Noetherian local ring \((R, \mathfrak m)\), a nonzero finite
484	  \(R\)-module \(M\), and an \(M\)-regular element \(x \in \mathfrak m\),
485	  \[
486	    \text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M/xM) + 1 \;=\; \text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M).
487	  \]
488	\end{lemma}
489	\begin{proof}
490	  \leanok
491	  Reduces to the Ext characterisation
492	  (\cref{lem:depth_via_ext}) on both sides; the short exact sequence
493	  \(0 \to M \xrightarrow{\,\cdot x\,} M \to M/xM \to 0\) gives a
494	  long exact sequence in \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\) on which
495	  multiplication by \(x\) is zero (since \(x \in \text{Ann}_R \kappa\)),
496	  breaking the LES into short exact pieces
497	  \(0 \to \text{Ext}^i(\kappa, M) \to \text{Ext}^i(\kappa, M/xM) \to
498	  \text{Ext}^{i+1}(\kappa, M) \to 0\). The translation to the
